speaker: interviewer
text: My name is [? Bernice ?] [? Krantz ?]. It is December the first, 1995. I'm conducting an interview with Leon Greenman. And we're in London, England.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had to present myself at half past 9:00 at [NON-ENGLISH] in Rotterdam, Department of the Alien Police. And I had to see Mr.-- inspector, Chief Inspector Roos, R-double O-S. So I present myself before half past 9:00. I remember sitting there. And in the hallway, I saw SS soldiers walking up and down. And then I saw Chief Inspector Roos. And he saw me. And he came like that. And I had to follow him into a room.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I sat opposite him. And he asked me various questions. And one of the questions was, what did your father do in the First World War? I said, '14, '18. I don't know, I said. I was only a child. I don't know. Now, my father was in intelligence service for the Allies in 1914, '18 war. That's all what I knew. But I was too young to, to be precise about that. So this man says, you Jews turn a question around the other way and don't want to answer, isn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: When he said that, I knew. I felt he was on the side of the Nazis. He was not on my side. So I had to be very careful. I said, I can't answer you. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, what are you, British or Dutch? You got a Dutch grandfather. I said, well, if you look in the book where I registered when I came in, all foreigners got to register. And I'm registered as an Englishman. Yes, he says. I know you're English. But I'm not going up the wall for you. I'm going to stamp you as a Dutch Jew. And he let me go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Half past 12, I was home and my wife all upset, grandmother upset. Because usually, you don't get, they don't let you go home. A lot of things like that happened. And a few months after that, things happened to us what happened to a lot of my friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before you go into that, those few months between not getting your British nationality back and being allowed to leave--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was happening within Rotterdam and Holland and the family? How did you cope for those few months?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I just waited and kept quiet and hoped, all the time hoping that the Swiss consulate would send me--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your passport.

speaker: interviewee
text: Documents that I'm British. Then I can say, I'm British. Nothing like that happened. But you know, I walked Rotterdam. Rotterdam was in ruins. The center of Rotterdam was gone. It was a bad, very bad sight. And you couldn't do your shopping. You had to do your shopping between 3:00 and 5:00. If you were before or after that time in a shop, you never know who gave you away. There were good Dutchmen, and there were bad Dutchmen. I had both, bad and good ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: What could we do? We couldn't go to the pictures. And we couldn't go to the parks nor the swimming baths. Couldn't go anywhere at all. You had to be in after 8:00 in the evening. And that was it.

speaker: interviewee
text: You lived. You dared not speak your mind to everybody, because you never knew who could give you away. I had Jewish and non-Jewish friends. But what I did do was I listened every evening on my radio-- at that time, I still had my radio-- to the BBC news bulletin. And then the next morning, I went to my circle of Jewish friends and non-Jews, if possible, and told them the latest news, how the war was going. And often, I came to the Jewish house, my friend's and got no answer. And then the neighbors next door looked out of the window or came to the street door and said, they're not in. They were taken away last night. And that went on and on and on. My best friends were taken away like that. If I think of the sons and daughters that were called up and the mothers, then were called up later on, and the mothers crying, hysterically crying, and fainting, and all that, a lot of those things I witnessed. And they were all acquaintances of mine. So time went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year are we now?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're now in 1942. Yes, 1942. We [? fought-- ?] I'm just thinking what, what I can tell you between '40 and '42-- the same things. I must mention this. I think it's important. One afternoon, my wife came home from shopping. And I didn't like the look on her face. And I asked her what was the matter. And she said, they told me this afternoon that the Nazis are gassing the Jewish people in Auschwitz. And I said, don't be silly, gassing. And I thought of a gas cooker. You can't put people in gas cookers. I said, forget about it. It, it, it's not like that. But it was like that. But we didn't know. I talked her out of that idea. On another occasion, before the war I was very patriotic for the royalty. Every year, I used to hang out the Union Jack on the king's birthday. And during the Nazi occupation, I didn't hang out my flag. And above us was living a lady and gentleman. He had a job at the Spanish consul. And I never looked, liked the look of this woman. I never liked the woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that day, one afternoon, I was coming home from my walk or what. I used to try to get something to earn. And she said, oh, Mr. Greenman. The flag is not hanging out today. It's the king's birthday, you know? Oh, [? I told ?] myself, she's getting at me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, well, the flag was dirty. And it's in the washing. And when it's clean, I'll hang it out again. And that's how I got out of that. See, I didn't trust anybody. You couldn't trust anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like, one of the Jewish booksellers on the market-- I used to go in the markets, in the stalls, and search and talk about books. And I was warned by somebody, don't talk to that man there. And he was a Jewish man. I said, why? He says, he's courting the daughter of a Nazi, of a Dutch fascist. So that was that. Now, whether he gave me away or not, whether now, things happened all of a sudden.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to give up our radios. I couldn't do the news no more, as I wanted to. But above us, the family living above us, was nice people, good people. She allowed me to come up there every evening between 5:00 and 6:00, switch on her radio, listen to the BBC news bulletin, so I could continue the next day talk, telling the news.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we tried to make the best of it. And then October the 8th arrived, 1942. About half past 10:00, a ring at the door. In the morning or the evening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Evening. How, who can that be now, I said to my wife, this late of a night? The child was asleep. We had the cot next to us. I built the cot for the girl, for the boy. I jumped out of bed. And I pulled a rope. And the street door opens. And the light of a torch was shining in my face. And I heard a man's voice shout out, Greenman. [SPEAKING DUTCH], Dutch. I said, yes. And up stormed two men, two policemen in uniform, black jackets, Wellingtons. And when they reached me at the top, they said, get dressed and come along. So into the living room we went. I said, I don't need to come along. I said, we're English. We're British. And I showed them my birth certificate and a letter from the consul. I said, they're working for me, I expect documents. He says, get dressed and come along. Don't argue about it. And while I was arguing with him, but I didn't want to, I was hoping my nationality would save me from being deported. The other one was going through my books, took some books underneath his arm and a valued watercolor from the wall of old Rotterdam. And I said, please, let those things be. They're my stock, my living. He said, you'll get them back when you come back. Of course, I never got them back. And I don't know who, who the man was. Well, grandmother was crying. My wife was crying. And the baby was certainly crying. And I had to give in. We got dressed, picked up our rolled-up blankets. We had them ready in case, just in case.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you all went.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went down the stairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the grandmother, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandmother had to come along. Now, usually if there's any non-Jews in the room with you, they also got to go. But my neighbors came down and consoled us a little. And the police let that. He didn't say nothing. And then we went down the stairs. And in front of the house was a coach. And in the coach were already some Jewish people and a six-foot-tall young Nazi, a swastika band around his arm. And as we got in, he told me, sit down and quiet. We sat down. And that went from street to street, collecting Jewish families. And when the coach was full, off we went through the streets, across the River Maas, on the other side of the Maas, of the river, it stopped on a piece of land. And on that land stood some large wooden huts, formerly used for goods from America, Europe. And in front of Hut 24, [SPEAKING DUTCH]-- that's Dutch for Hut 24, which it's called now. After 50 years, Rotterdam is coming to their senses. We must do something about remembering the Jews. We went. We stood. The coach stopped before that hut. Our luggage was slung out on the floor, on the ground. We had to pick them up and carry them inside. And as I came in, I saw hundreds of Jewish families, a lot of people I knew, and also about four or five SS soldiers. So I went to one of them and showed him my birth certificate and said to them, I'm English, and Swiss consul letter. He waved them away. Showed them one when you go to Westerbork. Westerbork is the transit camp in the north of Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] So we were wandering around among the people, saying hello to friends I knew. I still see the sad faces. Being on the committee of the, of the Jewish club, which had all the families, I still see the sad faces of people. Didn't know what to do, taken out of the homes. They had to leave everything behind. Well, two days later, on the 10th of October, we, we're put in a train. And that train went to Westerbork. Just before you go to the train, did they feed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Feed?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was nothing to feed. We had taken along a pillowcase with medicines in there. You had to take. It was a list which Jews could take along-- pills, powders, bandages, medicines.

speaker: interviewer
text: And some food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a few sandwiches we'd taken along with us, something on the quick. But otherwise, there was nothing there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about facilities? Nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were too short in Hut 24. Rotterdam had 13,000 Jews. 12,000 of them passed through [SPEAKING DUTCH] 24. Very, very few came back of them. And I'm one of the very few. At present, they're rebuilding everything there. They're going to name the streets after prominent Jewish people from Rotterdam. And they're renewing the whole lot now, after 50 years. Anyhow, we got in the train. And we went to Westerbork. But we had to stop at [SPEAKING DUTCH]. A little station. There was no rails from [SPEAKING DUTCH] to the actual Westerbork site, the camp site. There is a village called Westerbork. That's why they called Westerbork Camp, to that village name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we had to get out of the train. It was pouring rain, several hundreds of us, and a very, very muddy road. Your shoes stuck in the mud. Now, can you imagine the old people, such as grandmother, struggling along? My wife had a baby in the arm. And I had the rolls of blankets over my shoulders, and. But we got to the camp, five kilometers walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we were in Westerbork Camp. We had to get into a barrack. And we were registered.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were all still together at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Still together, yeah. Yeah. We were registered. So in you go. And there's a table there behind it and a typewriter with a woman, a man or a woman behind. And then you were registered. Greenman, Leon, Jew, English, British. Yes, I'm English, or born where English. Now, you had in Westerbork one barrack, which we called the [SPEAKING DUTCH], the English barrack, meant for the double nationalities, people who were born-- were born in America or in England but lived in Holland. They were put in that barrack. So I had to live in that barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your family, or just--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Now, you could-- you were not sleeping with your wife and child. You could see your wife after 10 o'clock every morning. And in the evening at half past 8:00, quarter 9:00, you had to say good night and go to your own barrack. So you, you didn't see much in the evenings. But during the day, according to the job I did-- and I'll tell you-- you could see your wife and child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the grandmother? With your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandmother was in a different barrack for the Dutch Jews. And Grandmother was only very-- very soon, she was deported. And I remember in the barrack where she was put, there was a, a, a, a wooden bed. And in the bed laid a little man, 101 years old, asleep. They were all sent away, grandmother as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So during the day, you set-- no. I was the, that was the first day. The second day, I had to pull out to do some work. I was assigned with another half a dozen men and a truck of bricks to go back to [DUTCH] and unload the bricks. And while I was unloading, I said, what are what are going to build with those things here? So they said, we're building an SS barrack. And SS meant to me Germany. And I wasn't going to work for the Germans. I hated them. So when I got back to Westerbork that evening, I went to the barrack of labor and told them, I'm an Englishman. I don't need to work for the Germans. I will not working with the Germans. But I had to work. Everybody works. So they made me [SPEAKING GERMAN]. [SPEAKING GERMAN] is a German word for someone who gets the food out of the kitchen for the other prisoners. For me, it was at five o'clock, get up, go to the kitchen, get the milk for the babies. At midday, 12:00, half past 12:00, go to the kitchen, get the food for the grownups, and in the evening, a meal. That was my work. So I had plenty of time to be with my wife and child. And that time was spent with other double nationalities, sitting in the barrack at the table and, and, and talking. What else could you do? Or walking outside. Sometimes I went with my wife. And somebody else, other mothers, looked after our baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that went on-- no. Right-- we were just then in Westerbork. And we had to be registered also with people. And these were German and Dutch men, like solicitors. Now, one German was Dr. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. [SPEAKING GERMAN] was a German solicitor who-- Jewish-- was also a prisoner in, in Westerbork. Had probably come over from Germany in, earlier.

speaker: interviewer
text: And just for the record, solicitor in America is a lawyer.

speaker: interviewee
text: A lawyer, yeah. Where you got lawyers and liars. So and there was also the famous Professor [? Myers ?], the number-one lawyer in Holland, who made the Dutch law, the civil, civil law. And those two were working for me, and for others, but for me, to get the right documents. So they had facilities to contact the police in Rotterdam, to contact the Swiss consul, all of this. And I remember, one day, Professor [? Myers ?] called me to his office. And he said, look, I received your telegram from Rotterdam, from the Alien Police, proving that you had British passport in 1927. I said, there you are. He said, but the Nazis want original passports. You haven't got them. But we're going to keep on trying. Now, in Westerbork, the chief of the administration for deportation was a German Jew, Kurt Schlesinger, a six-foot-tall man, plus, bald headed, a Hitler mustache, and well-set. And he was hated and feared in Westerbork. I asked him many times, Mr. Schlesinger, get us out of Westerbork. Place us in hands of the Red Cross. We're British. We don't need to be here. He never took any notice. Never took any notice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were in Westerbork. Life carried on. I saw people go away, who I knew, see them stepping into the train. And I never saw them again. They were off. Did you know where they were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were going to Auschwitz to work. That's what we thought.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were told they were going to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz. And there was also a camp called Mauthausen. They used that to frighten you if you did something wrong, Mauthausen-- terrible name. But we never heard of Birkenau. I never heard of Birkenau in Holland, in, in, in, in Westerbork-- Auschwitz, Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So time went on. I just want to mention that I had a book of Berlitz English language. And I sat at a table with a half a dozen Dutchmen, also double nationality, but who had been living a long time in Holland. And I was trying to teach them some conversation English. So I said, Allies come in. We can talk to them. That's what I dream.

speaker: interviewee
text: Time went on. And one morning, early morning, a mate of mine came and woke me. He says, they arrested 200 men in Rotterdam on the streets. 200 men, I told myself, maybe my father. So I jumped out of bed, dressed, and went to the barrack, barrack 51, the prison, the punishment prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to the barrack. I couldn't get in through the door. It was all chained up. So I climbed up the side of the barrack. And it was still dark. It was early morning. And side of the barrack, opened the window at the top, and I shouted down the name of my father. And what I could see was a lot of men and a lot of smoke. They were smoking. And yes, my father was there. He came to the window. And I was talking to him. Don't leave the barrack. I'll see you tomorrow morning. And I'll take you to Dr. [? Neuberger. ?] And we'll see about what can be done for us. And while I was talking, all of a sudden, I felt my leg being pulled. So I heard a voice say, [SPEAKING GERMAN], Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was the voice of Kurt Schlesinger. He must have been walking around there. So the man who pulled my leg, the assistant of Schlesinger, he said, this is the Englishman. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. So Schlesinger said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Come down, or I'll send you to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to my father, I'll see you tomorrow morning. I got to leave you. So I jumped down. And I stood in front of Schlesinger. I said, Mr. Schlesinger, you can't send me to Auschwitz. We're English. And he went. A few months went by. So you had contact with your father at that point? Do you know-- Yeah, my fath-- I took him out of the barrack. We walked in the, in the grounds in Westerbork and wondered-- he was registered as well. And we wondered what was going to happen. What happened to your mother at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: My own mother?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Forgot to tell you. I've got to go right back. I was two years and a half, young, when my mother died in Rotterdam. It appears that through the family, the heart failure, something. And my mother must have died as well. She was only allowed to have one child. And she brought six into the world. So probably, it didn't do any good to her. My father was left with six children. So he couldn't manage. And he took a housekeeper, a non-Jewish woman housekeeper. And he married her later on. And the fam--

speaker: interviewer
text: So she wasn't brought to Westerbork with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She wasn't. She wasn't Jewish. She probably saved my father's life. Because the Germans, later on, they took my father to Westerbork, as I told you. But then they thought-- and there was a law-- if a Jew was married to a non-Jewish or vice versa, they could see into the matter and, and don't, and not deport you. So they kept my father for a long time in Westerbork.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was sent home later on. But I didn't know that until later when I got freed. Now, where was I now? You were spending time talking to your father and walking in the grounds of Westerbork.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, wondering how [? and whom. ?] Terrible time. We didn't know what to do. We didn't know what to do. An outsider can't feel what, what was going on in our mind and in our hearts. Some of them took it light, but a lot of them didn't. The longer you could stay in Holland, the more chance you had that the war would end, and you would be freed, and all those things. You tried to fix yourself in a different frame. But it didn't work. And then it was the 17th, the 18th, January, 1943. After about four months in Westerbork, this is what happens. The lights go on in all the barracks. And on the table is a man with a sheet of paper in his hands, and on the paper the names of the people that going to be deported.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm laying there in bed, listening. And I look around, you know, underneath my blanket. I see them get dressed. That one gets dressed, all ready to go. When is it going to end?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I hear, Greenman, Leon. Get up. Get dressed. Tomorrow morning, deportation. And the same thing happens in the barrack with my wife and my child. So I got dressed. I rushed to Dr. [? Neuberger's ?] office. And I shows him-- you get a strip of paper, deportation.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, what? I don't understand, he says. And he talks to me. He says, your papers are on the way. They're supposed to come. Anyhow, nothing helped.

speaker: interviewee
text: At half past 8:00, we stood there. There were 750 people. The exact is 748 people, as registered in the books. But I'm naming it 750, easy reckoning. And I says to [? Els, ?] my wife, look, Schlesinger is talking to Konrad Gemmeker, the SS commander from Westerbork. Now, as we walk towards that, we stop. You stop. And we stop next to Schlesinger. And you say this time-- he, I said, he doesn't like me anymore. I've been troubling him. You tell him we're English. We need not go. And our documents are on the way. They can arrive any minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went. And we stopped at Schlesinger. And she said that. And he gave us less than a second. He said to Konrad Gemmeker, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. This has been refused in the Hague. They've got to go. We're going to Hague, the headquarter of the SS.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went through the gates into the train. This was still the passenger train. Later on, you get the cattle trucks. In the train we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the grandmother, she was still with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Our baby was ill. He had some [INAUDIBLE] in the head. Don't know. There's still alive in Rotterdam a doctor who took blood from me and injected it into my baby's [INAUDIBLE] try to-- because children were ill.
